title: Onion gravy
subtitle: Slow-caramelised, rich enough to carry a meal
excerpt: Onions cooked long and low until they collapse into a deep, sweet-savoury gravy. The kind that makes bangers and mash the dish it's supposed to be, not the sidekick version.
sourceNotes: Classic British gravy tradition, documented in Isabella Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, and in Nigel Slater's Kitchen Diaries (used as a general reference for proportions and method). The slow-caramelised onion base is a standard technique of British home cooking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months. Defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat gently, adding a splash of stock if it has thickened.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles or triples without issue. Make a large batch and portion into small containers for the freezer.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The onions can be caramelised up to 2 days ahead and kept in the fridge. Add the stock and finish the gravy when needed.

glossaryTerms.definition: To cook food slowly over low heat until its natural sugars break down and turn golden brown, developing a deep, sweet flavour.

content.text: The difference between a gravy that tastes of stock and one that tastes of something is usually about forty minutes of patience with an onion. The method is simple: low heat, a good amount of butter, and enough time for the onions to properly
. Rushed, they stay sharp and pale. Given time, they become soft, sweet, and deep-coloured — the base of a gravy worth making.

This is the standard British onion gravy: the one that goes with bangers and mash, with toad in the hole, with a sausage casserole that needs something to pull it together. It is not a jus. It is not a reduction. It is an onion gravy, which is to say it should taste primarily of onions, supported by stock and a little Worcestershire.
A large frying pan or wide sauté pan gives the onions room to spread and lose moisture faster than a cramped saucepan. The wider the surface, the quicker they caramelise.
Cook the onions low and slow

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} unsalted butter with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over the lowest heat you can manage. Add {{onion}} thinly sliced onions and the thyme sprigs if using. Stir to coat, then let them sit undisturbed for 5 minutes before stirring again. The goal is heat low enough that the onions steam gently in their own moisture before they begin to colour.

Continue cooking for 35 to 40 minutes, stirring every 5 minutes or so, until the onions are very soft, golden brown, and sweet-smelling. If the pan threatens to catch, add a splash of water and scrape the base. Do not rush this step; pale, undercooked onions produce a sharp, thin gravy.

Scatter {{plain-flour}} of plain flour over the onions and stir for 1 minute on the heat to cook out the raw flour. Remove the thyme sprigs. Pour in {{stock-beef}} of beef stock (or vegetable stock) in a steady stream, stirring constantly to prevent lumps. Add {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce and a good grinding of pepper.

Raise the heat to medium and cook, stirring occasionally, for 5 to 8 minutes until the gravy has thickened to a consistency that coats the back of a spoon. Taste and adjust salt. For a smoother result, press a ladle through a sieve — though leaving the onion strands in is the traditional finish and gives more body.

items.cause: Onions were not caramelised fully before adding the flour and stock
items.fix: Return to low heat with the onions and cook for another 15 minutes before proceeding. The colour and body come from the onions, not the stock.

items.cause: Stock has reduced too far, or too much flour was used
items.fix: Stir in extra stock a splash at a time until the consistency is right. It should flow freely when poured.

items.cause: Flour was added to wet onions and not stirred through evenly before the stock went in
items.fix: Press the gravy through a fine-mesh sieve, pressing the onions through with the back of a ladle. Reheat gently.

items.cause: Onions scorched rather than caramelised; heat was too high
items.fix: Avoid this by keeping the heat very low throughout. If it has happened, the bitterness cannot be rescued. Start again.

content.text: The caramelised onions keep in the fridge for 2 days — make them ahead and add the stock on the day. Finished gravy keeps refrigerated for 3 days; reheat in a saucepan over medium heat, stirring, with a splash of extra stock if needed. For the freezer, cool completely, pour into small containers, and freeze for up to 3 months. Defrost overnight in the fridge.

Onion gravy is one of the oldest elements of British home cooking: wherever there were onions and dripping, there was a gravy. Mrs Beeton's 1861 manual describes several onion sauces for roast meat, their logic unchanged from what appears here. The slow-cooked version became the standard accompaniment to sausages during the twentieth century, when bangers and mash evolved from a wartime economy meal into a dish with its own following.
